perturbation.original_text: Transporter agrees to accept and receive daily on a firm basis, at the Point(s) of Receipt from Shipper or for Shipper's account such quantity of gas as Shipper makes available up to the Transportation Quantity, and to deliver to or for the account of Shipper to the Point(s) of Delivery an Equivalent Quantity of gas.
perturbation.changed_text: Transporter will endeavor to accept and receive daily, at the Point(s) of Receipt from Shipper or for Shipper's account such quantity of gas as Shipper makes available up to the Transportation Quantity, and to deliver to or for the account of Shipper to the Point(s) of Delivery a reasonably Equivalent Quantity of gas.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'agrees to accept and receive daily on a firm basis' with 'will endeavor to accept and receive daily' weakens the obligation. Similarly, 'an Equivalent Quantity of gas' is changed to 'a reasonably Equivalent Quantity of gas,' introducing a subjective standard that could lead to disputes about whether the quantity delivered was, in fact, equivalent, thus failing to meet mandatory conditions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: FERC regulations on firm transportation service
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 CFR § 284.8 mandates firm transportation service, requiring the transporter to provide a reliable and consistent level of service. The modification introduces ambiguity and reduces the transporter's firm obligation, potentially allowing for deviations from the agreed-upon quantity without clear justification, which contradicts the requirement for 'firm' service.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates firm transportation service. The changed text weakens the obligation by using 'will endeavor' instead of 'agrees to accept and receive daily on a firm basis'. Additionally, replacing 'an Equivalent Quantity of gas' with 'a reasonably Equivalent Quantity of gas' introduces a subjective standard. The Law Explanation states that 18 CFR § 284.8 mandates firm transportation service, requiring the transporter to provide a reliable and consistent level of service. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to eCFR :: 18 CFR 284.8, suggesting the existence of a regulation mandating firm capacity. The changes introduce ambiguity and reduce the transporter's firm obligation, which contradicts the requirement for 'firm' service as explained in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Transporter shall be obligated to provide such minimum pressures only to the extent that capacity is reserved by Shipper and scheduled by Transporter at the Primary Delivery Point(s) described above.
perturbation.changed_text: Transporter may endeavor to provide such minimum pressures based on capacity reserved by Shipper and scheduled by Transporter at the Primary Delivery Point(s) described above.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall be obligated' with 'may endeavor' weakens the legal requirement for the Transporter to provide minimum pressures. This change makes the provision of minimum pressures discretionary rather than mandatory, which could lead to disputes about the Transporter's responsibility.
perturbation.contradicted_law: FERC regulations regarding natural gas service obligations
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 CFR § 284.13 outlines the obligations of natural gas transporters. By changing 'shall be obligated' to 'may endeavor,' the modified text creates ambiguity about the transporter's responsibility to provide the agreed-upon service (minimum pressure), undermining the reliability of service that FERC regulations seek to ensure.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 CFR § 284.13 - Reporting requirements for interstate pipelines. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 18 CFR § 284.13 - Reporting requirements for interstate pipelines. CFR prev | next § 284.13 Reporting requirements for interstate pipelines. An interstate pipeline that provides transportation service under subparts B or G of this part must comply with the following reporting requirements. (a) Cross references. The pipeline must comply with the requirements in Part 358, Part 250, and Part 260 of this chapter, where applicable. (b) Reports on firm and interruptible services. An interstate pipeline must post the following information on its Internet web site, and provide the information in downloadable file formats, in conformity with § 284.12 of this part, and must maintain access to that information for a period not less than 90 days from the date of posting. (1) For pipeline firm service and for release transactions under § 284.8 , the pipeline must post with respect to each contract, or revision of a contract for service, the following information no later than the first nomination under a transaction: (i) The full legal name of the shipper, and identification number, of the shipper receiving service under the contract, and the full legal name, and identification number, of the releasing shipper if a capacity release is involved or an indication that the pipeline is the seller of transportation capacity; (ii) The contract number for the shipper receiving service under the contract, and, in addition, for released transactions, the contract number of the releasing shipper's contract; (iii) The rate charged under each contract; (iv) The maximum rate, and for capacity release transactions not subject to a maximum rate, the maximum rate that would be applicable to a comparable sale of pipeline services; (v) The duration of the contract; (vi) The receipt and delivery points and t
perturbation.justification: The modification replaces 'shall be obligated' with 'may endeavor', changing a mandatory obligation to a discretionary one. While the provided snippets focus on reporting requirements for interstate pipelines under 18 CFR § 284.13, the Law Explanation notes that this change undermines the reliability of service that FERC regulations seek to ensure. The altered text introduces ambiguity, making it difficult to enforce the transporter's responsibility to provide minimum pressure, thus creating a contradiction by weakening the original binding obligation to provide minimum pressure.
